sources.name: Wikipedia — Battletoads
sources.notes: Character differences, level structure, mechanics overview.

sources.name: Speedrun.com — Battletoads (NES)
sources.notes: Speedrun community mechanics documentation including dash jump technique.

notes: Battletoads is a Rare action game notorious for extreme difficulty, especially Turbo Tunnel (Level 3).
DEFINING FEATURE: Multiple radically different gameplay modes within a single game — the paradigm shifts mid-game.
Players: Rash and Zitz (2-player co-op). Pimple is kidnapped and is the rescue target.
The toad bodies are comedically transformed during attacks — arms become hammers, feet become blades, etc.
Dash Jump: frame-perfect technique — sprint + jump on same frame. Enables faster traversal.
Most walk/jump values not precisely documented from ROM sources — estimated from gameplay.

rash.description: One of two default playable toads. Nimble, smaller frame.

zitz.description: Second playable toad. Slightly different attack animations.

pimple.description: The third toad — kidnapped at the game's start. Not playable in standard NES version.
pimple.notes: Pimple is the rescue objective, not a player character in the original NES game.

sprint.description: Double-tap direction to sprint. Faster than walk.
sprint.notes: Estimate. Sprint enables the strongest attack animations.

dash_jump.description: Frame-perfect technique: press jump on the exact same frame as the second tap of a sprint input.
dash_jump.effect: Allows sprinting while airborne — faster horizontal traversal than walk-jumping.
dash_jump.notes: Frame-perfect execution. Standard technique in speedruns.

combat._note: The toad bodies comically transform during attack animations — the defining visual hook of the game.

basic_punch.description: Standard punch. Transforms arm into enlarged fist.

kick.description: Kick attack. Transforms foot into large boot.
kick.notes: Different toad characters use kicks as primary vs secondary.

running_attacks.description: Special attacks only available while sprinting. Include body slams, headbutts, and spinning attacks.
running_attacks.notes: Running attacks are more powerful than standing attacks. Setting 'manual dash' in options allows faster access.

throws.description: Grab and throw enemies. Grabs are context-dependent.
throws.notes: Throwing enemies into other enemies is efficient crowd control.

wall_smash.description: Slam enemies into walls for additional damage.

level_modes._note: Battletoads' defining design choice: multiple completely different gameplay modes across 12 levels. THE PARADIGM SHIFTS CONSTANTLY.

beat_em_up_standard.description: Standard side-scrolling beat-em-up. Fight through enemies with melee combat.

turbo_tunnel.description: Auto-scrolling hoverbike section. Player must dodge walls at high speed. Infamously difficult.
turbo_tunnel.physics: Left/right dodge only. No standard jump physics. Instant response required.
notes: Considered one of the most difficult sections in NES history.
Requires precise memorization of wall patterns.
Auto-scroll speed increases as the level progresses.
A significant portion of players who attempt the game never pass this stage.

terra_tubes.description: Underwater swimming section with tube-navigation puzzles.
terra_tubes.physics: 8-directional swimming. No gravity. Air meter present (must surface periodically).
terra_tubes.notes: Drowning mechanics add urgency to navigation.

surf_city.description: Toads surf on boards down a river, dodging obstacles.
surf_city.physics: Constant forward scroll. Jump/duck to dodge overhead/ground obstacles.
surf_city.notes: Similar auto-scroll pattern to Turbo Tunnel but on water.

snake_pit.description: Toads climb poles that connect platforms. Enemies attack while climbing.
snake_pit.physics: Pole climbing physics — up/down speed, dismount timing.
snake_pit.notes: Spatial orientation challenge — attacked from multiple levels simultaneously.

clinger_winger.description: Toads grip an electric wire and pull themselves along a circuit while being chased.
clinger_winger.physics: Wire-gripping movement. Must stay ahead of the chaser.
clinger_winger.notes: Another memorization-heavy auto-scroll with instant death penalty.

volkmires_inferno.description: Toads fly a jet over an obstacle field.
volkmires_inferno.physics: 8-directional flight. Ground hazards below, aerial obstacles above.
volkmires_inferno.notes: Flying physics — free movement in 8 directions at constant altitude range.

design_notes_for_giants_drink: Battletoads is a textbook case of 'genre shifting within a single game.' Each level mode demands completely different skills: the player who mastered the beat-em-up sections must learn scrolling-obstacle physics from scratch for Turbo Tunnel. The game does not apologize for this — it demands mastery of each mode.

Turbo Tunnel's infamy reveals something interesting about difficulty spikes: a hard section early in a game acts as a filter, not just a challenge. Players either invest in mastering it or they don't. This creates a dedicated player base that shares a common credential (beating level 3).

The body transformation during combat is purely visual — the actual hitboxes are conventional. But the visual comedy reframes the violence: it's cartoonish, exaggerated, impossible. This tonal choice (violence as slapstick) makes the game's intensity feel playful rather than grim.

Battletoads' multiplayer creates a notorious problem: the game has friendly fire, and the second player can accidentally (or intentionally) kill the first. This created genuine social conflict between co-op partners and is a famous example of unintended emergent behavior in multiplayer design.

The multiple level modes are also a pacing tool: the transitions between modes break up the rhythm and prevent fatigue. After intense beat-em-up, a racing section feels like variety. After the racing section, the next combat section feels like relief. Variety as tension management.
